author: @michaelheurkens4538
text: Hello. I just wanted to add that you need to be sure that any solder that will touch water or food vessels should be lead (Pb)-free. Even if it doesn't touch food or water, just touching any lead residue can taint edibles even if just in trace amounts. Cool project! I'm an old "tin banger", so watch the fumes while soldering. Cheers from northern Alberta, Canada.

author: @mfsamuel
text: I solder onto steel when working with electronics (battery tabs, mounting posts, etc.) Short mention in the video, but I use zinc chloride made from the zinc oxide from old batteries dissolved in HCL. I have not tried this anything this large, but the zinc chloride might be a better option for some people.

author: @audiowan
text: Yet one more Great Video , as usual,     I was told in HVAC class when it comes to potable water;  to always  remember to use plumbers silver solder (must not contain lead ).  Try not to use electronics solder like  60Sn/40Pb  (means 60 percent tin and 40 percent lead).    I use SAFE FLO/OATEY  a mix of  tin, copper, silver and  (melts at around 232 C or 450F) ...    Happy Tea drinking..

author: @andrewwheeldon3027
text: This is pretty wild; I have never thought of soft soldering stainless before. I have been in the musical instrument trade all of my working life;  and was once involved in a BBC project "the scrapheap orchestra" where I was challenged to make 2 clarinets 2 flutes and a piccolo entirely from recycled materials to be placed at the proms in the albert hall! I digress... but I used recycled stainless-steel cutlery to make all the instrument keywork. It worked well and I silver soldered all of these together. Lots of people told me that silver soldering stainless was not a thing and couldn't be done!! Not only is that not true, I did it in an entirely conventional way with conventional materials and processes and it worked fine. I used a Jhonson and Mathey borax-based flux "easy flow" and regular silver solver and a propane torch. Joints were tested to destruction... I mention this because though you are right that with water cooling the joints of the soft soldered vessel you should encounter no problems; I can't help thinking that you would have a broader safety margin using an alloy which will stand 650-700 deg C and no special fluxes to you in my experience!

author: @dj1NM3
text:
I was wondering what sort of flux to use, because ordinary rosin does nothing on stainless steel and solder just beads straight off.
The only change I think might be a good idea is to use *stainless* steel wool, otherwise the surface you've scrubbed will go rusty from the iron particles you've scratched in the surface.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: now I am wondering for higher temp application can you use the phosphoric acid and copper carbonate flux, to do any of the types of brazing using a propane and oxygen torch the same way you can braze regular steel together?

replies.author: @dj1NM3
replies.text:
Possibly using a borax based flux, like Cigweld "Comweld GP Silver Brazing Flux", with high silver content silver-solder.
The risk with higher temp applications is your tin (or lead/tin, if its not for food) solder melting out.

replies.author: @richardlilley6274
replies.text:
Yeah I got one ' and they get stupid hot "
i'm concerned that if its not full to the top with water 
That any of the higher soldered joints will melt out and leak at next filling" spraying hot water and steam out..!
Dousing the fire
Or burning the cook

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @dj1NM3 but will the borax react to the passivation of the stainless here, the way his mixture of phosphoric acid/copper carbonate mix does? I know the right flux is needed for the right job, like the zinc chloride I believe my aluminum welding flux is made from, that may not work here, and probably will give poor performance on iron I am guessing, but works well with the aluminum brazing/soldering (though I would rather use the spin friction welding I have been using on both aluminum and titanium, since its all aluminum or titanium all the way through rather than different metals being wetted onto each other to give the bond.

replies.author: @dj1NM3
replies.text:
@ThomasAndersonbsf Yes.
GP silver brazing flux is used on most metals, except aluminium alloys.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: is the copper carbonate the "basic copper carbonate" like found in Malachite and verdigris? Or is it something different that has a method to making it?

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: Wikipedia shows a method of making a solution of copper sulfate and adding sodium bicarbonate. to make it, guessing it makes sodium sulfate and copper carbonate, and we have to do some fractional recrystallization to separate them?

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: ok never mind looks like the copper carbonate is insoluable in water while the sodium sulfate is soluble so washing with water seems the solution to getting rid of the sodium and sulfate, leaving the copper carbonate :)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: heh now I am wondering if a thin layer of the copper carbonate is hit with a laser will it leave a pure metal layer, or will it end up a bit spongy due to the carbon dioxide it is sure to release or worse still will it just convert into copper oxide powder?🤣

author: @salimufari
text: Won't the solder just melt the first time you try to boil water in that? I think you would need to tig weld this together to make it work properly.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: As long as it has a significant amount of water in it, it shouldn't reach temperatures enough to degrade the solder. Two things happen simultaneously--water can absorb a lot of heat energy, and then the phase change process of liquid to gas/vapor takes A LOT of heat out with it.

replies.author: @cyril3935
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering Dear Robert, hello from France.

Thank you very much for this clever recipe of soldering flux.
As a welding expert, I always recommend to keep as far as possible hydrochloric acid from any surface of stainless steel, because chlorine ions, when its concentration is above 50 ppm, is able to "dig" some crevices on most stainless steel like 304L.

It's an autocatalytic corrosion mechanism, where the very thin layer of chrome dioxide on the surface CANNOT rebuild, because of the presence of a "viscous cap" which contains dissolved brown Fe2+ ions + OH- anions, with the very acidic Cl- anion and hydrogen protons which are on the very bottom of the crack, digging it ...until total path done in the thickness !!

After sometimes few hours or days, we can observe "pitting craters" and also "water streams" pissing litterally like "Jets d'eau des jardins de Versailles".

The numerical value for the resistance against pitting corrosion cracking is called PREN : Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number.
Most stainless steels like 304L & 316L are very poor (circa 7, sometimes 10).

We use huge amounts of Molybdenum & Nitrogen additions to enhance this number, up to 35, 40, 45.

I was wondering... an old recipe made by you, from some years ago : could it be possible to reupload/redo your recipe of coppering gel ? (made of gelatin, copper-I oxide and formic acid in microwave ? Such recipe could be very helpful for making some heat exchangers.

Once the copper layer is made in surface, it could be easy to apply an eutectic melange of phosphorus with copper, by using an slight oxygen rich flame. Cu+P alloy wire allow to make them melt first, thus avoiding to make some performation of base material when heating.

It creates some fumes with phosphorus oxidation. The delay of heating allows the Phosphorus-copper filler material to melt first. Then the degassing/evaporation of phosphorus allows the remaining copper to become richer in copper alone, by heating it after making the soldering.
After few seconds, it becomes closer from the ground, pure copper layer.

Without such copper layer prior to soldering, the highly refractory (and invisible layer) of chromium dioxide appears, especially when heating, thus forbids any bonding/soldering of foreign materials.

Beware of overheating, because liquid copper is able to pass through the grains of the stainless steel (most often it's austenite, less often it's ferritic stainless steel with very huge grains growth when heated). Heating stainless steel with molten solder can creating some "hot cracking phenomena", by the phenomenon of liquation. 

A concentration in the very middle of the welded joint of a low-temp melting material, is prone to abrupt cracking. Because of the high stress resulting by heating such materials with HUGE dilatation coefficients. Austenitic stainless steels are prone to ~ 3,5 times more lineic dilatation, than carbon steels.

Always keep in mind that a straight welding will be stressed TWICE than a circular welding. If a pressure buildup appears, it's always such vertical in your case, these "longitudinal welds" that will crack first under pressure buidup.

Hohohow, by the way, beware about some tin alloys which contains lead.

It is a highly neurotoxic metal, and it ease considerably the risk of hot cracking / poor mechanical yield.
Always prefer

binary Sn96%+Cu4% (light bronze)
or
ternary Sn90%+Cu8%+Ag2%
or
binary Sn96%+Ag4% (electronics compliant to RoHS regulation).

Best regards and thank you so much for your ideas & realizations.
